papers.title: P. succinatutens: Succinate Scavenging & Pathogen Inhibition
papers.abstract: P. succinatutens scavenges luminal succinate through its obligate succinate-to-propionate metabolic pathway, depriving opportunistic pathogens (Enterobacteriaceae) of a critical carbon source while simultaneously lowering luminal pH via propionate production. The methylmalonyl-CoA pathway is fully validated in vitro and in murine mono-colonization models (Level 3). Core enzymes (ScpA/ScpB/ScpC) show 100% amino acid sequence identity between two Phascolarctobacterium type strains. In porcine models, this function reaches only Level 1 (co-occurrence network inference). Critical evidence gap (Level 4 void): porcine-derived strain isolation and mono-colonization in weaned piglets is entirely absent.
papers.modelSystem: In vitro + Murine mono-colonization (L3); Porcine co-occurrence network (L1)
papers.keyLimitation: No porcine-derived strain isolated; no mono-colonization in piglets performed. Translational bottleneck: murine L3 to porcine L4.
papers.forwardJustification: P. succinatutens succinate-to-propionate pathway fully characterized in vitro. Methylmalonyl-CoA pathway enzymes (ScpA/ScpB/ScpC) 100% conserved. Murine mono-colonization validates causal propionate production.
papers.reverseJustification: No evidence for host control of P. succinatutens abundance or activity. Reverse pathway entirely uncharacterized in any model species.
papers.summary: Benchmark case for the translational gap. P. succinatutens succinate-to-propionate pathway is mechanistically validated at L3 in mice but only inferred from co-occurrence networks at L1 in pigs. Represents the critical evidence void at the murine-to-porcine translational interface. B12-dependence of the methylmalonyl-CoA pathway creates a functional intersection between the probiotic and Vitamin B12 nodes.

papers.title: P. succinatutens: Intestinal Barrier Maintenance via Propionate
papers.abstract: P. succinatutens maintains intestinal barrier integrity through propionate via a dual-pathway mechanism: (1) membrane pathway: GPR43/GPR41 activation rerouting signals through beta-arrestin and cAMP branches to block NF-kappaB nuclear translocation; (2) intracellular pathway: HDAC inhibition relieving epigenetic repression of tight junction proteins (ZO-1, occludin). In murine models, the complete causal chain of mono-colonization to propionate production to TLR4/NF-kappaB inhibition to barrier maintenance is established (Level 3). Two Level 2 studies in pigs independently validated propionate-mediated TLR4/NF-kappaB suppression and tight junction protein upregulation. Key limitation: porcine evidence validates propionate's barrier-protective effect but does not demonstrate that P. succinatutens produces sufficient propionate in situ for this effect.
papers.modelSystem: Murine mono-colonization (L3); Porcine dietary fiber fermentation models (L2)
papers.keyLimitation: Strain-specific causal validation missing in pigs: propionate's barrier effect validated, but P. succinatutens' in situ propionate production capacity in porcine gut not demonstrated.
papers.summary: Best-validated probiotic function node. Dual-pathway propionate signaling mechanism fully characterized. Translational gap: strain-specific propionate production quantification in porcine gut lumen and epithelium needed to advance from L2 to L3 in pigs.

papers.title: P. succinatutens: Lipid Deposition Reduction via MYC-Arginine-FOXO3a Axis
papers.abstract: P. succinatutens reduces host lipid deposition through the propionate-mediated MYC-arginine-FOXO3a molecular axis: HDAC inhibition leads to MYC transcriptional attenuation, derepression of glutamine transporters (SLC1A5/SLC38A5) enhances de novo arginine biosynthesis, and arginine accumulation suppresses PI3K/Akt/FOXO3a signaling to reduce adipogenic gene expression. Murine FMT and mono-colonization experiments have fully validated this pathway (Level 3). In porcine models, UCP1 knock-in pigs show negative correlation between fecal SCFAs and adipogenic gene expression (Level 1 association). The pathway is not P. succinatutens-specific in the porcine context. Functional divergence from P. faecium (which operates via TLR2) constitutes genus-level metabolic specialization.
papers.modelSystem: Murine FMT + mono-colonization (L3); Porcine UCP1 knock-in correlation (L1)
papers.keyLimitation: Pathway validated in mice only; porcine evidence is SCFA-adipogenic gene correlation, not P. succinatutens-specific. Pathway conservation across commercial pig breeds is speculative.
papers.summary: Demonstrates genus-level functional divergence (P. succinatutens vs P. faecium). MYC-arginine-FOXO3a axis is a potential target for lipid reduction in livestock. Porcine validation remains entirely at association level.

papers.title: Butyrate/SCFAs: Compartmentalized Co-Metabolism Node (Priority P1)
papers.abstract: Butyrate is the most extensively validated node in the compartmentalized co-metabolism network. Forward pathway (microbial metabolites to host epigenetics): Level 2b in vivo validation in porcine models. Xiong et al. (2016) landmark study demonstrated butyrate upregulates endogenous host defense peptides via HDAC inhibition in weaned piglets. Porcine ileal organoid experiments confirmed HDAC inhibition and barrier-enhancing effects. Tibetan pig studies showed butyrate promotes macrophage H4K31 crotonylation via mTORC1-HDAC3/8. Reverse pathway (host epigenetics to microbiota): only Level 1. Host gene regulatory networks (PRDM15, STAT1) and germ-free pig transcriptome data provide hypothesis-generating clues. Coupling: Level 0. Forward and reverse effects never simultaneously observed in a single experimental system. Measurement Depth: Level 0. All studies measured only a single compartment (luminal) at a single timepoint. The three-order-of-magnitude concentration difference between luminal (mM) and intracellular (muM) butyrate has not been incorporated into experimental design.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine in vivo (weaned piglets, Tibetan pigs) + ileal organoids
papers.keyLimitation: Forward pathway at L2b but reverse pathway at L1 and coupling at L0. The fundamental butyrate concentration gradient (lumen mM vs epithelium muM) has never been incorporated into experimental design. No study has simultaneously measured forward and reverse effects.
papers.summary: Most-validated co-metabolism node. Forward pathway well-characterized at L2b in pigs (HDAC inhibition, host defense peptides, crotonylation). But reverse pathway, coupling verification, and multi-compartment measurement all at L0-L1. Represents the core evidence asymmetry in the co-metabolism framework.

papers.title: Bile Acids: Host-Microbiome Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Bile acids are a porcine-validated core node. Forward pathway: Level 2b. Hyodeoxycholic acid (HDCA), the dominant porcine bile acid, enhances intestinal barrier function via FXR-PI3K/AKT pathway in weaned piglets, increasing Lactobacillus abundance from 5.28% to 37.97%. Neonatal piglets show bile acid-mediated epithelial proliferation and barrier maturation through TGR5 activation and FXR downregulation. Substantial species differences in bile acid pool composition (pigs: HDCA-dominant; mice: CA/DCA-dominant) limit murine evidence extrapolation. Reverse pathway: only Level 1. Whether host bile acid signaling alterations feed back to regulate microbial community composition has not been causally validated. Coupling: Level 0. FXR/TGR5 activation and microbial restructuring never simultaneously tracked. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Main evidence gap: concentration gradient data for bile acids at the lumen-epithelium interface are absent.
papers.keyLimitation: Species-specific bile acid composition (HDCA-dominant in pigs) renders murine data non-directly translatable. Reverse pathway causal validation void is the critical bottleneck for understanding bile acid-microbiota feedback circuits.
papers.summary: Porcine-validated forward pathway with species-specific bile acid (HDCA). Lactobacillus expansion from 5.3% to 38.0% is the strongest microbial community shift documented in any node. Reverse pathway (host-to-microbiome bile acid signaling) is the critical evidence gap. FXR-dependent antimicrobial peptide pathway recently identified as potential reverse mechanism.

papers.title: Tryptophan Metabolites: AhR Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Tryptophan metabolites are partially porcine-validated. Forward pathway: Level 2a (preliminary in vivo). Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron alleviates weaning diarrhea in piglets via tryptophan metabolism-AhR-Nrf2 signaling. Gut microbiota-mediated tryptophan metabolism suppresses pyroptosis via AhR signaling to maintain intestinal epithelial homeostasis in piglets. Dietary tryptophan supplementation modulates gut microbial composition and activates AhR signaling. Validations are based on specific bacterial strains or dietary interventions, not direct causal validation of tryptophan metabolites per se. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Tryptophan metabolite reverse regulatory role completely unexplored. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gap: concentration gradients of tryptophan metabolites (indole, kynurenine) across lumen-epithelium-microenvironment compartments never simultaneously measured. Temporal asynchrony between AhR activation and microbial community shifts uncharacterized.
papers.keyLimitation: Validations are strain-specific (B. thetaiotaomicron) or dietary, not direct metabolite causal validation. AhR activation and pyroptosis suppression not linked causally in porcine gut in vivo. Reverse pathway entirely unexplored.
papers.summary: AhR signaling is the central mechanism linking tryptophan metabolites to intestinal homeostasis. Strain-specific (B. thetaiotaomicron) and dietary intervention evidence at L2a. Direct metabolite causal validation, reverse pathway, and multi-compartment measurement all at L0.

papers.title: Polyamines: Cross-Species Extrapolation Node (Priority P3)
papers.abstract: Polyamines are a cross-species extrapolated predicted node with no direct validation evidence at any level in porcine models. Forward pathway: only Level 1. All existing evidence derives from murine models and in vitro studies: polyamines regulate histone methylation and acetylation by promoting nucleosome condensation; polyamines and gut microbiota form bidirectional interactions maintaining intestinal homeostasis (including microbial growth and biofilm formation). None of these mechanisms have been experimentally verified in the porcine gut. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gaps: baseline concentration and spatial distribution data for polyamines in the porcine gut are entirely absent; polyamine biosynthetic capacity of porcine-derived strains uncharacterized; polyamine transporter expression patterns in porcine intestinal epithelium unknown.
papers.modelSystem: Murine models and in vitro only
papers.keyLimitation: No porcine data at any level. Baseline polyamine concentrations in porcine gut unknown. Porcine-derived polyamine-producing strains not isolated. Entirely dependent on cross-species extrapolation from murine evidence.
papers.summary: Weakest evidence base among all five nodes in porcine context. All evidence is murine-derived. Inclusion based on established mammalian host-microbe co-metabolic functions. Porcine functional relevance requires de novo establishment. First essential step: baseline polyamine concentration measurement in porcine gut compartments.

papers.title: Vitamin B12: One-Carbon Metabolism Node (Priority P3)
papers.abstract: Vitamin B12 has the weakest evidence base among all five nodes. Forward pathway: only Level 1 (association inference). B12 is a key cofactor in host one-carbon metabolism, participating in S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) synthesis to provide methyl donors for DNA methylation. This theoretical link is elaborated in reviews but direct causal validation between B12 concentrations and DNA methylation marks is absent in porcine models. In P. succinatutens' succinate-to-propionate pathway, B12 is the essential coenzyme for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase. B12 availability directly impacts propionate production efficiency, representing the functional intersection with Project 1. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gaps: B12 concentration distribution across porcine gut compartments unknown; B12-propionate metabolic coupling never validated in situ in porcine gut; causal effect of B12 on porcine intestinal epithelial DNA methylation untested.
papers.modelSystem: Theoretical/review-level only; no direct experimental validation
papers.keyLimitation: Weakest of all five nodes. Entirely at theoretical/review level. No experimental data on B12-DNA methylation link in porcine gut. B12-propionate coupling in situ completely untested. P. succinatutens B12 dependence is the only functional anchor in the co-metabolism network.
papers.summary: Weakest evidence node but critical for understanding one-carbon metabolism integration with microbial SCFA production. Functional intersection with P. succinatutens (methylmalonyl-CoA mutase requires B12) provides the mechanistic rationale for inclusion. Entirely a hypothesis awaiting experimental testing in porcine models.

papers.title: Propionate: Succinate-to-Propionate Pathway Node (Priority P1)
papers.abstract: Propionate is a major microbial SCFA in the porcine gut, produced via the succinate pathway (dominant in Bacteroidetes and Phascolarctobacterium) and the acrylate pathway (dominant in Firmicutes). Forward pathway (microbial propionate → host): Level 2b in pigs. Propionate activates GPR41/GPR43 in porcine intestinal epithelium, suppresses HDAC activity, and serves as a gluconeogenic substrate in the porcine liver. The P. succinatutens methylmalonyl-CoA pathway directly couples succinate consumption to propionate production, representing a B12-dependent metabolic intersection. Reverse pathway (host → microbial propionate): Level 1. Dietary fiber composition and host bile acid secretion patterns influence propionate-producing community structure, but direct host genetic control over propionate output is uncharacterized. Key evidence gap: propionate concentration gradients across the porcine gut epithelium have never been systematically mapped. Bidirectional coupling remains at Level 0.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine dietary intervention + in vitro bacterial cultures
papers.keyLimitation: Propionate concentration gradients across gut compartments unmapped. P. succinatutens in situ propionate production rate in porcine gut unknown. Reverse pathway entirely at association level. B12-propionate coupling never measured in vivo.
papers.summary: Propionate is the second-most abundant SCFA in the porcine hindgut and a core output of the succinate-to-propionate pathway. Wraps P. succinatutens metabolism (succinate→propionate), B12 cofactor requirement (methylmalonyl-CoA mutase), and host GPR41/43 signaling into a single integrated node. Forward pathway partially validated at L2b in pigs; reverse pathway and coupling entirely unexplored.
papers.forwardJustification: Propionate-GPR41/43 signaling validated in porcine epithelium. HDAC inhibition and gluconeogenic roles characterized. P. succinatutens pathway fully mapped in vitro.
papers.reverseJustification: No causal evidence for host control of propionate-producing community in pigs. Dietary fiber → propionate association established but no host genetic or immune mechanism characterized.

papers.title: Acetate: Cross-Feeding & Systemic Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Acetate is the most abundant SCFA in the porcine gut (typically 50-60% of total SCFAs) and the primary substrate for butyrate-producing bacteria via cross-feeding. Forward pathway (microbial acetate → host): Level 2a in pigs. Acetate crosses the gut epithelium via MCT1/SMCT1 transporters, enters the portal circulation, and reaches peripheral tissues at high concentrations (100-200 muM in human portal blood; porcine data limited). Acetate activates GPR43 on enteroendocrine L-cells, stimulates GLP-1/PYY secretion, and serves as a lipogenic substrate and epigenetic modifier (acetyl-CoA for histone acetylation). Reverse pathway (host → acetate producers): Level 1. Host diet composition and intestinal transit time influence acetogenic community structure, but host-driven feedback mechanisms are unexplored. Key evidence gap: the gut microbial acetate economy in pigs — production rates, cross-feeding fluxes, and epithelial transport kinetics — lacks systematic quantification.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine dietary fiber models (association level); Murine mono-colonization (causal)
papers.keyLimitation: Acetate cross-feeding network structure in porcine gut unknown. Portal acetate concentration data absent for pigs. Acetate contribution to host histone acetylation in porcine intestinal epithelium never quantified. Reverse pathway entirely at theoretical level.
papers.summary: Acetate is quantitatively dominant but mechanistically understudied in pigs. Its role as the primary cross-feeding substrate for butyrate producers makes it a critical upstream node in the SCFA network. Porcine-specific portal and peripheral acetate kinetics are a major data gap. Histone acetylation link offers a direct epigenetic readout for microbial acetate activity.
papers.forwardJustification: GPR43 activation and GLP-1/PYY secretion by acetate validated in murine models. Acetate-to-butyrate cross-feeding well-characterized in vitro. Porcine evidence limited to dietary fiber → acetate correlations.
papers.reverseJustification: No evidence for host-directed control of acetogenic community composition. Host diet as driver established; host immune/genetic feedback unexplored.

papers.title: Folate/B9: One-Carbon Metabolism & Methyl Donor Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Folate (vitamin B9) is a key microbial product in the gut that participates in host one-carbon metabolism alongside B12. Gut bacteria (especially Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus species) are net folate producers, contributing 10-30% of host folate requirements. Forward pathway (microbial folate → host): Level 1 in pigs. Microbial folate is absorbed in the colon via reduced folate carrier (RFC) and proton-coupled folate transporter (PCFT), contributing to host methyl donor pools (SAM cycle) and nucleotide synthesis. Bifidobacterium adolescentis folate production has been demonstrated in vitro and in murine models, but porcine in vivo evidence is absent. Reverse pathway (host → microbial folate): Level 1. Host dietary folate intake and the host folate absorption efficiency set the competitive landscape for microbial folate producers vs. auxotrophs. In pigs, dietary folic acid supplementation alters gut microbial composition but the mechanism (direct vs. indirect via host metabolism) is unclear. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Key gap: gut luminal folate concentrations and microbial folate production rates in pigs are completely unknown.
papers.modelSystem: Murine Bifidobacterium mono-association (L2a); Porcine dietary folic acid (L1 association)
papers.keyLimitation: Porcine gut luminal folate concentrations unknown. Microbial folate production rates in pig gut never measured. Bifidobacterium/Lactobacillus folate contribution to host methyl donor pool unquantified in any large animal model. B12-folate metabolic intersection in porcine gut unexplored.
papers.summary: Folate represents the microbial contribution to host one-carbon metabolism at the methyl donor level. Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus are the primary porcine-relevant folate producers. The B12-folate intersection in one-carbon metabolism is mechanistically established in human/murine systems but untested in pigs. Luminal concentration and production rate data are the prerequisite measurements.
papers.forwardJustification: Microbial folate production demonstrated in Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus in vitro. Colonic folate absorption via RFC/PCFT characterized in rodents. Porcine evidence: dietary folic acid changes gut microbiota composition at association level only.
papers.reverseJustification: Host dietary folate status influences competitive dynamics between folate producers and auxotrophs. Porcine dietary folic acid → microbiota shift observed but mechanistic pathway (direct microbial vs. host-mediated) not resolved.

papers.title: Vitamin A/Retinoic Acid: Immune-Microbiome Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Vitamin A and its active metabolite retinoic acid (RA) sit at the host-microbiome interface, regulating both host immunity and gut microbial ecology. Forward pathway (microbe-influenced RA → host): Level 2a in pigs. Gut bacteria influence host vitamin A metabolism through: (1) microbial production of retinaldehyde dehydrogenase (RALDH) cofactors that modulate host RA synthesis; (2) microbial SCFAs promoting dendritic cell RALDH expression and RA production. Porcine weaning stress decreases intestinal RALDH activity, correlating with microbial community disruption. Reverse pathway (host RA → microbiome): Level 2b in pigs. Retinoic acid directly regulates the gut microbiome through: (1) RA-induced epithelial IL-22/STAT3 signaling altering AMP secretion profiles; (2) RA-RAR signaling in B cells promoting IgA class switching, which shapes microbial composition; (3) RA modulation of Treg/Th17 balance influencing immune tolerance to commensals. This reverse pathway is better characterized than most nodes because the RA-RAR axis is well-studied. Coupling: Level 0 — RA effects on host immunity and microbial composition never tracked simultaneously in pigs. Measurement Depth: Level 0.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine weaning stress models (L2a); Murine RAR knockout + gnotobiotic (L3)
papers.keyLimitation: Simultaneous measurement of RA-dependent host immune activation AND microbial community shifts not performed in pigs. Porcine intestinal RA concentration and RALDH activity spatial mapping absent. The RA-RAR-IgA axis relevance to porcine gut commensal control unquantified.
papers.summary: Vitamin A/retinoic acid is the best-characterized REVERSE pathway node. The RA-RAR-IgA axis provides a mechanistically detailed host-to-microbiome signaling route. The forward pathway (microbes → host RA metabolism) is less characterized, creating an unusual reverse-dominant evidence asymmetry. Key AhR-RA crosstalk at the tryptophan-vitamin A metabolic intersection is unexplored in pigs.
papers.forwardJustification: Porcine weaning stress reduces intestinal RALDH activity (enzyme-level evidence). Microbial SCFA promotion of DC RALDH expression established in mice. Causal microbial → RA → host immune effect not demonstrated in pigs.
papers.reverseJustification: RA-RAR signaling in B cell IgA class switching well-characterized (molecular pathway). RA-IL-22-STAT3-AMP axis established. Porcine dietary vitamin A alters gut microbiota composition. Strongest reverse pathway evidence among all non-Bile Acid nodes at L2b.

papers.title: P. succinatutens: Porcine Gut Strain Isolation & Characterization Status
papers.abstract: Phascolarctobacterium succinatutens is an obligate succinate-consuming, propionate-producing bacterium in the family Acidaminococcaceae. It occupies a keystone metabolic niche in the porcine gut: its succinate-to-propionate pathway prevents luminal succinate accumulation (which would otherwise fuel Enterobacteriaceae blooms) while producing propionate for host signaling. Current isolation status: YIT 12066 and YIT 12067 were isolated from human feces (Yakult Central Institute, 2012). NO porcine-derived strain has been isolated to date. 16S rRNA gene surveys place Phascolarctobacterium at 1-5% relative abundance in healthy pig feces across diverse breeds (Yorkshire, Landrace, Duroc, Meishan). Metagenomic binning has recovered near-complete P. succinatutens genomes from pig fecal metagenomes. Core metabolic pathway (methylmalonyl-CoA) shows 95-100% amino acid sequence identity between human and porcine metagenome-assembled genomes. Key bottleneck: without a porcine-derived isolate, mono-colonization experiments in piglets cannot be performed, blocking L3→L4 translational advance.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine 16S/metagenomic surveys (L1); Human isolate in vitro (L2); Murine cross-colonization (L3)
papers.keyLimitation: No porcine-derived P. succinatutens isolate exists — the single most important bottleneck for advancing the translational pipeline from murine L3 to porcine L4. Without a pig-derived strain, causal validation in piglets is impossible.
papers.summary: P. succinatutens is the highest-priority strain for porcine isolation. Its succinate-to-propionate pathway is the mechanistic center of the co-metabolism framework. The human isolate (YIT 12066/12067) provides a genetic template, but porcine strain isolation is the prerequisite for advancing the entire Project 1 translational pipeline from L3 to L4. Core metabolic pathway is conserved but host-specific colonization factors remain unknown.
papers.forwardJustification: Succinate-to-propionate pathway fully characterized in human isolates. Methylmalonyl-CoA pathway enzymes (ScpA/ScpB/ScpC) 95-100% conserved in porcine MAGs. In vitro propionate production demonstrated. Host signaling effects inferred from propionate node.
papers.reverseJustification: Host dietary and immunological factors influencing P. succinatutens abundance in pig gut completely uncharacterized. Porcine intestinal factors driving niche specialization unknown. Dietary succinate availability is the only characterized host input.

papers.title: Lactobacillus: Porcine Gut Probiotic Ecology Node (Priority P1)
papers.abstract: Lactobacillus species (including Lactiplantibacillus, Limosilactobacillus, and Ligilactobacillus per reclassified taxonomy) are dominant small intestinal colonizers in pigs, reaching 10^7-10^9 CFU/g in the duodenum and jejunum. They are the most extensively studied porcine gut bacteria, with >200 published porcine feeding trials. Forward pathway (Lactobacillus → host): Level 2b in pigs. Multiple porcine trials demonstrate Lactobacillus-mediated pathogen inhibition (E. coli K88, Salmonella Typhimurium), intestinal barrier enhancement (ZO-1, occludin upregulation), and immune modulation (TLR2/TLR4 signaling, IgA promotion). However, strain-level specificity is high — effects observed with one strain (e.g., L. plantarum JDFM LP11) do not generalize. Reverse pathway (host → Lactobacillus): Level 2a in pigs. Host dietary fiber composition, weaning stress, and intestinal mucin glycosylation patterns all influence Lactobacillus community composition. Host antimicrobial peptide secretion (RegIII-gamma) selectively suppresses Lactobacillus in a porcine weaning model. Key gap: strain-level resolution is required — genus-level 16S data obscures functional divergence between species/strains.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine in vivo (weaned piglets, >200 trials) + porcine intestinal organoids
papers.keyLimitation: Genus-level data dominate; strain-level functional divergence is critical but under-characterized. Reclassified taxonomy (Lactobacillus → 25 genera, 2020) not reflected in most porcine literature. Strain-specific colonization factors and persistence in porcine gut poorly understood.
papers.summary: Lactobacillus is the most extensively studied porcine gut bacterium but suffers from nomenclatural fragmentation and inadequate strain-level resolution. Forward pathway evidence at L2b (barrier, immune, pathogen inhibition), reverse pathway at L2a (host AMPs and weaning stress). Represents the best-characterized bidirectional system among all strain nodes but lacks coupling (never measured simultaneously in same animals).
papers.forwardJustification: Extensive porcine trial literature: barrier enhancement (ZO-1/occludin), pathogen inhibition (E. coli, Salmonella), immune modulation (TLR2/4, IgA). Strain specificity high — effects not generalizable across Lactobacillus species.
papers.reverseJustification: Porcine weaning stress alters Lactobacillus community structure. Host RegIII-gamma AMP selectively suppresses Lactobacillus in piglets. Dietary fiber → Lactobacillus bloom established. Host mucin glycosylation influence demonstrated at L2a.

papers.title: Prevotella: Porcine Hindgut Dominance & Succinate Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Prevotella is the dominant Gram-negative genus in the porcine hindgut, often representing 15-30% of the fecal metagenome — substantially higher than in humans. Prevotella species are major succinate producers in the pig colon, making them the primary upstream metabolic partner for succinate-consuming bacteria (P. succinatutens). Forward pathway (Prevotella → host): Level 2a in pigs. Prevotella-dominated enterotypes are associated with higher luminal SCFA concentrations and improved feed efficiency in pigs. Prevotella copri produces succinate as a fermentation end-product, which is then cross-fed to propionate producers. Specific Prevotella species encode carbohydrate-active enzymes (CAZymes) targeting porcine dietary fibers (arabinoxylan, pectin). Reverse pathway (host → Prevotella): Level 2a. Host dietary carbohydrate composition is the dominant driver of Prevotella abundance. Porcine host genotype (MUC4, FUT1 polymorphisms) influences Prevotella colonization. Key gap: Prevotella-to-P. succinatutens succinate cross-feeding has never been directly measured in the porcine gut in situ. The succinate intermediate pool size and flux rate are unknown.
papers.keyLimitation: Prevotella-to-Phascolarctobacterium succinate cross-feeding never measured in situ in porcine gut. Succinate intermediate pool size unknown. Prevotella species-level functional diversity in pigs poorly characterized — genus-level data mask substantial metabolic heterogeneity.
papers.summary: Prevotella is the dominant succinate producer and primary metabolic partner for P. succinatutens in the porcine hindgut. The Prevotella→succinate→Phascolarctobacterium→propionate cascade is the central metabolite flow in the co-metabolism framework. Host dietary fiber composition is the dominant top-down driver of Prevotella abundance. Porcine-specific Prevotella CAZyme repertoire and succinate production kinetics are key unknowns.
papers.forwardJustification: Prevotella-dominated enterotype → higher SCFA and improved feed efficiency in multiple porcine cohorts. P. copri succinate production characterized in vitro. Porcine Prevotella CAZyme diversity documented at metagenomic level. Direct succinate flux to propionate producers not measured.
papers.reverseJustification: Host dietary carbohydrate composition is the dominant driver — well-established in multiple porcine dietary intervention trials. Host genotype (MUC4, FUT1) influence documented. Weaning transition shifts Prevotella abundance, driven by dietary change.

papers.title: Bifidobacterium longum alleviation of metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease: A multi-omics landscape of microbiota and metabolome reconfiguration.
papers.abstract: The gut microbiome-host metabolism axis plays a critical role in the pathogenesis of metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD). Although the probiotic Bifidobacterium longum (B. longum) shows promise in ameliorating metabolic disorders, its functional impact on the microbiome-metabolome interplay in MASLD remains elusive. Herein, we established a MASLD mouse model using a high-fat, high-fructose (HFHF) diet and conducted integrated multi-omics analyses, including liver tra

papers.title: Gut Microbiota-Derived Propionate: A Potential Therapeutic Target for Diabetic Retinopathy via Regulating the Gut-Retina Axis.
papers.abstract: Diabetic retinopathy (DR), a prominent microvascular impairment arising from diabetes, causes substantial visual dysfunction. Emerging evidence indicates that intestinal dysbiosis promotes DR progression via the gut-retina axis. Short-chain fatty acids serve a critical function in regulating gut microbiota, and propionate, a vital member of them, has potential translational value in DR prevention and management through the gut-retina axis. papers.title: Quercetin regulates lipid metabolism and inflammatory microenvironment of microglia after spinal cord injury through activating the butyrate-FFAR2/AMPK/TFEB gut-brain axis signaling pathway.
papers.abstract: Spinal cord injury (SCI) leads to a microenvironment destruction characterized by inflammation, lipid metabolism dysregulation, and impaired tissue regeneration, collectively impeding functional recovery. Recent studies have shown that the gut-brain axis is an important pathway for regulating the microenvironment after SCI. Quercetin (QUE) is a natural flavonoid with powerful anti-inflammatory and metabolic regulation properties, showing promising prospects in promoting SCI repair. This study ai

papers.title: Dietary baicalin supplementation enhances growth performance in fattening Hu sheep via dual modulation of immunity and gastrointestinal microbiome-metabolic crosstalk.
papers.abstract: Baicalin is a bioactive flavonoid from Scutellaria baicalensis Georgi with antioxidant, anti‑inflammatory and antibacterial properties. However, its bitter taste and susceptibility to ruminal degradation limit its practical use in mammals. Enteric coating technology might overcome these limitations by enabling targeted intestinal release. This study investigated effects of dietary supplementation with baicalin and coated baicalin on rumen fermentation, gastrointestinal microbiota, immune functio
papers.journal: Journal of animal science and biotechnology

papers.title: Codonopsis pilosula as the sovereign herb in Shenling Baizhu formula ameliorates DSS-induced ulcerative colitis via spermidine-AMPK-mediated mitophagy to inhibit the cGAS-STING inflammatory axis.
papers.abstract: Dextran sulfate sodium-induced ulcerative colitis is featured by excessive intestinal inflammation and disrupted mucosal immune homeostasis. Codonopsis pilosula is the sovereign herb in Shenling Baizhu Formula for ulcerative colitis therapy, and Codonopsis pilosula polysaccharides are its key active components, while their immunomodulatory effects and molecular mechanisms in Shenling Baizhu Formula against ulcerative colitis remain elusive. papers.title: Lactobacillaceae in Acute and Chronic Liver Diseases: From Microbiota Modulation to Therapeutic Potential.
papers.abstract: Liver diseases, including metabolic-associated fatty liver disease (MAFLD), alcoholic liver disease (ALD), and viral hepatitis, are highly prevalent and constitute a major global health burden. Accumulating evidence indicates that dysbiosis of the gut microbiota is closely associated with the development and progression of various liver diseases. Among microbial regulators, Lactobacillaceae, a family of probiotic lactic acid bacteria, has attracted considerable attention for its capacity to resh

papers.title: Choline counteracts pregnancy undernutrition-associated colitis in sheep by restoring microbiota driven bile acid metabolism.
papers.abstract: High-fertility sheep experience undernutrition during late gestation, which induces body metabolic disruption and intestinal inflammation. Crucially, the gut microbiota-bile acid axis is a promising intestinal inflammation target, but how choline modulates this axis in livestock enteritis is unknown. This study investigated choline's protective mechanisms against undernutrition-induced intestinal inflammation in sheep, focusing on gut microbiota and bile acid regulation. Choline consistently all

papers.title: Item recognition is associated with gut microbiota composition in healthy humans.
papers.abstract: Murine studies show that the gut microbiota-the collection of the microbes residing in the large intestine-affects memory performance in the host. However, whether commensal gut bacteria are linked to human episodic memory remains unknown. Here, we investigated whether individual differences in episodic memory performance were associated with differences in the indigenous gut microbiota composition between individuals. We show that greater gut microbiota α diversity was associated with better it

papers.title: Atractylodes lancea Polysaccharides Protect against Staphylococcus aureus-Induced Lung Injury by Remodeling Gut Microbiota and Restoring Tryptophan Metabolism.
papers.abstract: Gut microbiota and their metabolites may shape pulmonary immune responses and infection progression. Plant-derived polysaccharides, such as Atractylodes lancea polysaccharides, have shown prebiotic activity, yet their protective mechanisms in pneumonia remain unclear. Here, A. lancea polysaccharides (ALPs), a water-soluble acidic fraction mainly composed of arabinose, galacturonic acid, glucose, and galactose with a predominant molecular weight of 3.52 kDa, were evaluated in a murine model of St
papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: Ethanol-Tolerant Lactobacillus rhamnosus L7 Ameliorates Alcohol-Induced Liver Injury in Mice by Inhibiting the TLR4/MyD88/NF-κB Inflammatory Signaling Pathway and Restoring Gut Microbiota Homeostasis.
papers.abstract: Alcohol-related liver disease (ALD) represents a growing global public health concern, and there is still a lack of safe, effective clinical intervention approaches to date. This study aimed to evaluate the protective effect of ethanol-tolerant Lactobacillus (L.) rhamnosus L7 on ALD in mice. In this work, L. rhamnosus L7 with high ethanol tolerance was isolated from fermented grains and pit mud. The strain was intragastrically gavaged to mice with alcoholic liver injury established via the Gao-b

papers.title: Integrated Genomic, Structural, and Metabolomic Profiling of Freeze-Dried Postbiotic Supernatants Reveals Multi-Target Anticancer Activity Against Caco-2 Cells.
papers.abstract: Postbiotic metabolites derived from probiotics bacteria have gained increasing attention as multitargeted anticancer agents with improved safety and stability compared to live microorganisms. In this study, we evaluated the anticancer activity of freeze-dried cell-free supernatants (CFS) obtained from Bifidobacterium bifidum and Lacticaseibacillus rhamnosus against human colorectal adenocarcinoma (Caco-2) cells and integrated functional, molecular, and omics-based analyses. Cytotoxicity assessme

papers.title: Strain-Specific Lactic Acid Bacteria Fermentation of Kiwifruit Juice: From Flavor Optimization to In Vitro Gut Microbiota Modulation.
papers.abstract: The deep processing of kiwifruit remains an urgent challenge. In this work, we evaluated the effects of seven lactic acid bacteria (LAB) strains on the overall quality of the kiwifruit juice. All strains exhibited robust growth, and LAB fermentation markedly improved the flavor profile of the juice, with the M29 group best preserving the characteristic kiwi fruit aroma. Sensory analysis further revealed that M29 and LB22 particularly enhanced the fruity sensory notes. In vitro fecal fermentation
papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: The Role of Microbiome-Associated Metabolites and Their Clinical Implications in Traumatic Brain Injury: A Scoping Review.
papers.abstract: Traumatic brain injury (TBI) is a major public health challenge, with heterogeneous mechanisms and limited targeted therapies. Despite advances in neurocritical care, interventions to meaningfully alter long-term outcomes have been elusive, and treatment remains largely supportive. Parallel to this, increasing evidence from both preclinical models and human studies implicates the gut microbiome as a dynamic modulator of neurologic injury and recovery through the microbiome-gut-brain axis, a bidi

papers.title: Aging of the adaptive immune system affects the gut microbiome and systemic levels of vitamin B6.
papers.abstract: Age-associated dysregulation of the gut microbiota is a hallmark of aging and has been linked to multiple age-related diseases, yet upstream host factors driving these changes remain incompletely defined. Extensive bidirectional crosstalk between gut microbiota and mucosal immunity has been described. Aging is accompanied by a progressive decline in immune function, collectively termed aging-associated immune remodeling (AAIR). AAIR encompasses widespread compositional and functional changes tha

papers.title: Age and sex dependent shift in murine gut microbiome reveals pathological links to host cardiovascular and metabolic pathways.
papers.abstract: The gut microbiome undergoes dynamic, sex-dependent changes across the lifespan. However, comprehensive studies examining the combined effects of age and sex are limited. This study investigated both compositional and functional alterations in the gut microbiome of young (4 months, n = 14) and aged (20 months, n = 20) C57BL/6 J mice of both sexes using 16S rRNA gene (V3-V5) sequencing. Microbial community structure and predicted functional profiles were analyzed via QIIME2 and PICRUSt2, with pat

papers.title: The Prebiotic 2'-Fucosyllactose Ameliorates Colitis via an Akkermansia muciniphila-Mediated Prebiotic-Microbiota-Metabolite Axis.
papers.abstract: Recent studies indicate that 2'-fucosyllactose (2'-FL), a human milk oligosaccharide, alleviates inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) by modulating the gut microbiota, yet the underlying mechanisms and key functional bacteria involved remain unclear. This study employed fecal microbiota transplantation and an antibiotic-treated mouse model to investigate the role of 2'-FL-mediated gut microbiota in lipopolysaccharide-induced colitis, with a focus on Akkermansia muciniphila (A. muciniphila). Results
papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: Intracellular acidification by microbiota-derived valeric acid facilitates trans-kingdom ecology limiting Candida parapsilosis colonization.
papers.abstract: In hematopoietic cell transplant (HCT) patients, intestinal Candida parapsilosis expansion and translocation can cause life-threatening candidemia, yet whether commensal intestinal bacteria prevent Candida expansion remains incompletely defined. Here, we trained a machine learning model on supernatant metabolomic profiles of Lachnospiraceae to identify bacteria-derived inhibitors of fungal growth, identifying valeric and butyric acids as top hits. Fecal samples from HCT patients supported this a

papers.title: Study on the Alleviating Effect of Tropical Psidium guajava Basal Postbiotics on Hyperuricemia.
papers.abstract: Hyperuricemia (HUA) is a growing global health concern with a younger onset trend. Using a high-purine diet-induced HUA mouse model, this study evaluated kidney, colon, and gut microbiota damage and investigated the effects of Psidium guajava basal postbiotics (PGP). PGP reduced xanthine oxidase activity, modulated purine metabolism, and increased beneficial gut bacteria (Akkermansia muciniphila) and short-chain fatty acids (acetate, butyrate, propionate). This led to decreased blood urea nitrog
papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: Longitudinal associations of the gut microbiome with arterial stiffness in US adults: findings from the Baltimore Longitudinal Study of Aging.
papers.abstract: The gut microbiome affects arterial stiffness in experimental murine models; however, evidence in human longitudinal studies is lacking. In this study, we investigated longitudinal between-person (average) and within-person (change) associations of microbiome features with arterial stiffness. We assessed the fecal microbiome using whole genome metagenomic sequencing, and arterial stiffness using carotid-femoral pulse wave velocity (cfPWV). Our analytic sample consisted of 349 adults from the Bal

papers.title: Role of the gut microbiota-bile acid axis in liver fibrosis based on microbiomics and targeted metabolomics.
papers.abstract: Effective diagnoses and treatments for liver fibrosis (LF) are lacking. The gut microbiota (GM)-bile acid (BA) axis is critically associated with LF development and may represent a potential target for delaying or reversing LF.

papers.title: Gut-bone axis in rheumatoid arthritis: microbiota-driven barrier dysfunction, immune crosstalk, and therapeutic strategies.
papers.abstract: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic autoimmune disease characterized by persistent synovial inflammation and progressive bone destruction in which immune dysregulation plays a central role. Recent evidence has highlighted the gut-bone axis as a critical framework linking gut microbiota to skeletal and immune homeostasis. Gut microbiota dysbiosis disrupts intestinal barrier integrity by altering tight junction proteins and increasing intestinal permeability, facilitating microbial translocatio

papers.title: Gut microbiota-derived Tryptophanol driven by N-Carbamylglutamate alleviates premature ovarian failure through inhibiting oxidative stress.
papers.abstract: Premature ovarian failure (POF) affects 1-5% of women under 40 years old and is characterized by granulosa cells (GCs) apoptosis and follicular atresia. However, the pathogenesis of POF is complex and lacks effective prevention and treatment strategies. N-carbamylglutamate (NCG), a bioactive substance known for its antioxidant properties; however, whether it can alleviate POF remains unclear.

papers.title: Phenotypic, Genomic, and In Vivo Characterization of a Host-Derived Limosilactobacillus fermentum RLF77 with Potential to Prevent Post-weaning Diarrhea in Rabbits.
papers.abstract: Following the 2020 ban on antibiotic growth promoters in animal feed, diarrheal disease in weaned rabbits has become an increasingly important challenge for the rabbit industry. Due to their superior intestinal adaptability and colonization potential, host-derived probiotics are considered promising alternatives. In this study, Limosilactobacillus fermentum RLF77, a lactic acid bacterium, was isolated from the intestinal contents of healthy young rabbits. Strain RLF77 showed a favorable safety p

papers.title: Re-arming checkpoint blockade in MSS colorectal cancer: A precision-microbiome playbook from mechanisms to clinic.
papers.abstract: Immune checkpoint blockade transforms outcomes for the 15% of colorectal cancers (CRCs) with mismatch-repair deficiency; yet most tumours remain refractory. Beneficial gut microbes can change this. Akkermansia muciniphila, Bacteroides fragilis, and short-chain fatty acid producers prime dendritic cells to produce interleukin (IL)-12, polarise Th1 cells, and reinvigorate CD8+ T-cells. Antibiotics, Western-style diets, and Fusobacterium nucleatum foster myeloid suppression and β-catenin- or IL-17-

papers.title: Acid-enhanced drinkable hydrogel co-delivering riboflavin-loaded nanozymes for orchestrated inflammation-immune modulation and redox homeostasis in colitis therapy.
papers.abstract: Conventional inflammatory bowel disease therapies are limited by inefficient colon-targeted delivery, systemic side effects, and a failure to rectify the underlying oxidative stress and immune dysregulation. Riboflavin (RAF) possesses ideal antioxidant and immunomodulatory properties for colitis therapy but suffers from rapid proximal absorption. While acid-degradable metal-organic framework (MOF) nanozymes can co-deliver RAF and catalytically eliminate reactive oxygen species (ROS), they lack c

papers.title: Substrate recognition and cleavage by mucin degrading O-glycopeptidases from the gut microbe Bacteroides caccae.
papers.abstract: O-glycopeptidases are enzymes that hydrolyze the peptide bonds in glycoproteins by a mechanism that involves specific recognition of O-linked glycans on the substrate. Bacteroides caccae, an accomplished mucin degrader, is a member of the human gut microbiota with sixteen genes encoding putative O-glycopeptidases in the peptidase_M60 family. At present, the diversity of substrate selectivity in O-glycopeptidases is not well-understood nor is the rationale behind their expansion in bacteria such

papers.title: Microbiota-derived short-chain fatty acids mediate Candida albicans gastrointestinal colonization resistance.
papers.abstract: The gut microbiota plays critical roles in constraining Candida albicans colonization of the gastrointestinal (GI) tract, which is a key precursor to disseminated fungal infection in immunocompromised hosts. Depletion of commensal microbiota, such as by antibiotic treatment, increases C. albicans burden and promotes dissemination, yet the mechanisms of colonization resistance remain unclear. Here, we show that microbiota-derived short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) directly inhibit C. albicans growth

papers.title: Gut pathobionts translocate into liver and reshape intrahepatic microbiome to facilitate hepatocellular carcinoma.
papers.abstract: The role of intrahepatic microbiome in hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) remains elusive. Here, we profiled matched gut and intrahepatic microbiomes from HCC patients and healthy subjects. Compared to healthy subjects, we observed increased gut-liver microbiome similarity and a gut pathobionts-centred network in HCC, implying microbial transfer via gut-liver axis. Consistently, HCC stool transplantation to germ-free mice impaired gut barrier function and increased bacterial load in liver. Multi-sit

papers.title: Bifidobacterium breve207-1 protects against later-life colitis following early-life antibiotic exposure by enhancing intestinal barrier and immune homeostasis.
papers.abstract: The early colonization of bacteria has a significant impact on immune system development. The disruption of gut microbiota by antibiotics in early life is a major risk factor for pediatric inflammatory bowel disease. This study explores how Bifidobacterium breve 207-1 modulates gut microbiota and immune responses after early antibiotic exposure, and whether such intervention mitigates DSS-induced colitis later in life.

papers.title: Bifidobacterium breve PRL2020 mitigates antibiotic-induced gut symptoms and dysbiosis in children treated with amoxicillin or amoxicillin-clavulanate.
papers.abstract: Diarrhea caused by amoxicillin (A) or amoxicillin-clavulanate (AC) is common in children, and the induced dysbiosis is often characterized by a severe reduction in bifidobacteria. However, bifidobacteria are extremely antibiotic-sensitive, and their co-administration with antibiotics is likely unwise. The B. breve PRL2020 strain is an A- and AC-resistant probiotic. In a multicenter, randomized, controlled, non-profit study, we tested the tolerability and efficacy of the PRL2020 strain when co-ad
papers.journal: International microbiology : the official journal of the Spanish Society for Microbiology

papers.title: Clockwork symbiosis: The vital interplay of microbiota and human chronobiology.
papers.abstract: The human microbiota and host circadian system engage in a bidirectional dialogue with consequences for metabolism, immunity, and barrier physiology. This narrative review examines the microbiota-chronobiology interface across the gut and selected extra-intestinal niches and uses the term "clockwork symbiosis" as an organizing framework for this crosstalk. A structured, non-exhaustive search of PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, Cochrane Library, and Embase was conducted for studies published betwe

papers.title: Gut microbiome-blood cholesterol crosstalk: towards personalized strategies for dyslipidemia.
papers.abstract: Hypercholesterolemia is a major risk factor for cardiovascular diseases, influenced by both genetic predisposition and multifactorial acquired factors, including diet, lifestyle, obesity, type 2 diabetes, and gut microbiota dysbiosis. Accumulating evidence suggests that the gut microbiome plays a causal role in cholesterol metabolism through multiple complementary mechanisms, including bile acid transformation, modulation of hepatic and intestinal receptors (FXR, TGR5), production of short-chain

papers.title: Lacto-N-biose Improves Acute Colitis in Mice by Modulating Gut Microbiota and Metabolites.
papers.abstract: Lacto-N-biose (LNB) as the core structure among human milk oligosaccharides remained insufficiently characterized in terms of the potential to ameliorate colitis. This study investigated effects of LNB in colitis by using dextran sulfate sodium salt to induce mouse model. Results demonstrated that LNB significantly ameliorated the increase in disease activity index, suppressed immune factors TNF-α, F4/80, IL-6, and IFN-γ, increased factors IL-4 of colitis mice, and the high-dose group exhibited
papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: Multi-omics elucidates the regulatory mechanisms of tryptophan in gut health of weaned piglets.
papers.abstract: Tryptophan (Trp), an essential amino acid (AA) implicated in diverse physiological and pathological processes, remains incompletely characterized in its mechanisms regulating intestinal health in weaned piglets. In this study, 27 weaned Bama miniature pigs with highly homogeneous genetic characteristics (6.200 ± 0.242 kg) were randomly divided into three groups and fed a basal diet, a diet supplemented with 0.5-fold Trp, or a diet supplemented with 1.5-fold Trp for 21 days. papers.title: Chenodeoxycholic acid redirects the bile acid synthetic pathway to limit pro-inflammatory neutrophil infiltration and alleviate colitis.
papers.abstract: Patients with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) exhibit a dysregulated bile acid pool, characterized by increased primary and decreased secondary bile acids, largely due to gut microbiota dysfunction. However, the impact of colitis on hepatic bile acid synthesis remains poorly understood. In this study, analyses of public datasets, in-house patient samples, and an animal model revealed that colitis enhances flux through the classical bile acid synthesis pathway while suppressing the alternative p
papers.journal: Molecular therapy : the journal of the American Society of Gene Therapy

papers.title: Uric acid promotes dietary lipid absorption through microbiome and metabolomic remodeling via a liver-gut endocrine axis.
papers.abstract: Hyperuricemia closely correlates with obesity, yet high uric acid (UA) is largely viewed as a consequence or biomarker of obesity rather than a causal driver. Integrating human clinical analyses with animal studies, we redefine UA as a liver-derived endocrine regulator that drives obesity by modulating gut ecology. UA remodels the gut microbiota, selectively depleting Lactobacillus johnsonii by disrupting peptidoglycan synthesis. This depletion reduces microbial phenyllactic acid (PLA)-a metabol

papers.title: The gut-brain-gonad axis mediates salinity adaptation in an invasive turtle: causal evidence from microbiota transplantation and metabolite supplementation.
papers.abstract: Preliminary studies have shown that salinity stress can impair the differentiation of spermatogonial stem cells (SSC) in red-eared slider (Trachemys scripta elegans) through the gut-brain-gonad axis, thereby affecting their reproductive ability. However, a direct causal link between salinity-induced gut microbiota alterations and reproductive suppression remains unclear. To test the hypothesis that gut microbiota and their metabolites mediate salinity adaptation by modulating the gut-brain-gonad

papers.title: Microbiota-gut-brain axis and treatment resistance in epilepsy: a multicentre prospective study protocol (CARE).
papers.abstract: Approximately one-third of people with epilepsy (PWE) experience resistance to treatment, including pharmacological therapies, epilepsy surgery, vagus nerve stimulation (VNS) and dietary interventions such as the ketogenic diet (KD). Emerging evidence suggests that the gut microbiota may influence seizure susceptibility and treatment response through the microbiota-gut-brain axis, potentially contributing to treatment resistance. The MiCrobiota-gut-brain Axis in Resistant Epilepsy project invest

papers.title: Early Gut Microbiome Alterations in Mild Cognitive Impairment Reflect Changes in Alzheimer Disease.
papers.abstract: Alterations in the gut-brain axis have been increasingly linked to neurodegenerative diseases, including Alzheimer disease (AD). It remains unclear whether these microbiome changes are already present during early cognitive decline. We examined whether gut microbiome alterations characteristic of AD are detectable in mild cognitive impairment (MCI) and whether these changes follow a similar pattern across the cognitive continuum.

papers.title: Expanding vaginal microbiome pangenomes via a custom MIDAS database reveals Lactobacillus crispatus accessory genes associated with cervical dysplasia.
papers.abstract: The vaginal microbiome plays a central role in reproductive health. Vaginal microbiome dysbiosis is associated with many adverse reproductive health outcomes, but most studies have focused on associations at the species level. The potential contribution of intraspecies microbial variation, especially gene content differences across bacterial strains, remains underexplored in reproductive health contexts. papers.title: Microbiota dysbiosis influences immune system and muscle pathophysiology of dystrophin deficient mice.
papers.abstract: Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) is a progressive, severe muscle-wasting disease caused by mutations in DMD, encoding dystrophin, that leads to loss of muscle function with cardiac/respiratory failure and premature death. Since dystrophic muscles are sensed by infiltrating inflammatory cells, and gut microbial communities can cause immune dysregulation and metabolic syndrome, we sought to investigate whether intestinal bacteria support the muscle immune response in the mdx dystrophic murine mod

papers.title: Sodium butyrate induces cuproptosis by regulating the HDAC1-SLC31A1 axis in hepatocellular carcinoma.
papers.abstract: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is the primary type of liver malignancy, which stands as the third leading contributor to cancer-related death worldwide. Sodium butyrate (NaB), a gut microbiota-derived short-chain fatty acid with known anticancer activity, remains poorly understood in HCC. This study aimed to elucidate the effects and underlying mechanisms of NaB-induced cuproptosis in HCC cells. 200 mg/kg NaB inhibits HCC tumor growth by 73.9% and induced a 1.83- and 6.83-fold increase in the Cu
papers.journal: Food and chemical toxicology : an international journal published for the British Industrial Biological Research Association

papers.title: Gut-brain axis crosstalk in patients with Alzheimer's disease and high body mass index: Gut dysbiosis, blood-brain barrier disruption, and neuroinflammation.
papers.abstract: BackgroundAccumulating evidence indicates that body mass index (BMI) is related to Alzheimer's disease (AD).ObjectiveThis study aimed to investigate the impact of high BMI on cognitive function in AD patients and potential mechanisms involving gut dysbiosis, blood-brain barrier (BBB) disruption, and neuroinflammation in brain.MethodsA total of 50 AD patients were recruited and divided into AD with normal BMI (AD-nBMI) and AD with high BMI (AD-hBMI) groups. papers.title: The gut-brain axis in Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases: a systematic review of microbiota-derived biomarkers and novel therapeutic approaches.
papers.abstract: The altered gut microbiota substantially impacts the onset and progression of Alzheimer's disease (AD) and Parkinson's disease (PD), the two most widely studied neurodegenerative conditions. Microbiome-derived metabolites have been increasingly associated with disease onset, progression, and therapeutic targets in neurodegenerative disorders. Exploring the diagnostic and therapeutic implications of gut microbiome-derived biomarkers is critical to advancing our understanding and management of neu
papers.journal: Journal of clinical and experimental neuropsychology

papers.title: Ameliorative effects and mechanisms of Inonotus hispidus on PCOS via regulation of gut microbiota-ovarian metabolism axis and ferroptosis inhibition.
papers.abstract: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a prevalent endocrine metabolic disorder with limited therapeutic options. This study investigated the ameliorative effects and underlying mechanisms of the water extract of Inonotus hispidus (WE) on PCOS in a testosterone propionate induced rat model. The results of this study indicate that WE significantly reduced abnormal body weight gain, restored disrupted estrous cycles, and ameliorated ovarian cystic degeneration, interstitial fibrosis, and hepatic stea
papers.journal: The Journal of steroid biochemistry and molecular biology

papers.title: The gut microbiota alleviates depression by remodeling gut-brain energy metabolism.
papers.abstract: Although peripheral-brain crosstalk regulates energy metabolism, its role in depression remains unclear. Here, we used metabolic profiling to reveal elevated fecal creatine alongside reduced plasma and cerebrospinal fluid creatine in both patients with depression and mouse depression models. Exogenous creatine produced antidepressant-like effects mediated by gut microbiota. Bifidobacterium pseudolongum was identified as a significantly reduced gut bacterial species in depression, correlating wit

papers.title: Guggulsterone as a Modulator of the Microbiota-Bile Acid-FXR Axis: Molecular Mechanisms and Therapeutic Potential in Metabolic Ageing.
papers.abstract: Cholesterol biosynthesis and bile acid metabolism are tightly controlled by the Farnesoid X receptor (FXR), which integrates bile acid signaling with lipid, glucose, and inflammatory pathways to maintain metabolic homeostasis. Gut microbiota dysbiosis alters bile acid composition, increasing secondary bile acids that can chronically overactivate FXR, contributing to inflammatory bowel disease, metabolic syndrome, and liver-related disorders. papers.title: TGR5 is essential for protecting from chronic stress-induced learning and memory impairments in mice by modulating inflammation associated with the gut-brain axis.
papers.abstract: CS impairs brain function, causing long-term changes in neural systems linked to anxiety, depression, and cognition. TGR5 is a key receptor crucial for modulating various physiological processes, impacting metabolic homeostasis and inflammation. However, the role of TGR5 in regulating CS-induced learning and memory impairments along the gut-brain axis remains incompletely understood.

papers.title: Commensal Dysbiosis Alters Primary Bile Acid Signaling to Drive Mammary Gland Inflammation and Breast Tumor Dissemination.
papers.abstract: Breast cancer is the most commonly diagnosed malignancy and a leading cause of cancer-related mortality. Hormone receptor-positive (HR+) tumors represent the most prevalent metastatic subtype, and early dissemination remains a major clinical challenge. Commensal dysbiosis, defined as an inflammatory gut microbiome with low biodiversity, promotes metastasis by inducing mammary gland inflammation. Here, we investigated systemic mechanisms governing dysbiosis-induced metastasis. Metabolomic profili

papers.title: Aspartame promotes intestinal tumor progression via Akkermansia muciniphila -mediated 5-hydroxytryptophan production.
papers.abstract: Aspartame, one of the most widely consumed artificial sweeteners globally, has been associated with increased cancer risk in epidemiological studies, yet the underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study investigated the effects of subchronic aspartame exposure on intestinal tumorigenesis using the Apcmin/+ spontaneous intestinal tumor mouse model and elucidated the mechanistic pathways involved. Mice were exposed to physiologically relevant doses of aspartame (0.07 and 0.2 mg/mL) i

papers.title: Tryptophan 2,3-dioxygenase deficiency attenuates dextran sodium sulphate -induced ulcerative colitis in mice by suppressing macrophage polarisation and reducing intestinal epithelial cell apoptosis.
papers.abstract: Ulcerative colitis (UC) is a chronic, nonspecific inflammatory bowel disease. The kynurenine pathway (KP) of tryptophan metabolism has been implicated in several autoimmune diseases, including multiple sclerosis and rheumatoid arthritis. However, little is known about the role of KP in UC. This study investigated the role of tryptophan 2,3-dioxygenase (TDO2), a key rate-limiting enzyme of KP, in UC.

papers.title: Ferulic acid ameliorates metabolic dysfunction associated with fatty liver disease by regulating macrophage polarization through spermidine derived from gut microbiota.
papers.abstract: This study aimed to investigate the efficacy and mechanism of Ferulic acid (FA) in a high-fat diet (HFD)-induced murine model of Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD), focusing on the gut microbiota-polyamine-macrophage polarization axis.
papers.journal: The Journal of pharmacy and pharmacology

papers.title: Pediococcus acidilactici PA53 Improves Emotional Well-Being and Gut Health while Modulating Microbiota Composition in Older Adults: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial.
papers.abstract: The gut-brain axis links intestinal microbiota with emotional health, a critical concern in aging populations experiencing gut dysbiosis.

papers.title: A probiotic shield: Lactobacillus acidophilus immobilized in bacterial cellulose for a more sustainable release process and improved viability.
papers.abstract: Probiotics, defined as live microorganisms that confer a health benefit on the host when administered in adequate amounts, often require protective strategies to ensure their viability in food products. Immobilization in biopolymeric matrices presents a promising approach for enhancing bacterial resilience. This study focused on the fabrication of bacterial cellulose films as a platform for immobilizing Lactobacillus acidophilus. The physicochemical properties of the immobilized probiotic were i
papers.journal: Journal of the science of food and agriculture

papers.title: Adjuvant Lactiplantibacillus plantarum Lp05 modulates gut microbiota and alleviates symptoms during Helicobacter pylori eradication.
papers.abstract: Helicobacter pylori eradication therapy is often accompanied by gastrointestinal adverse effects and gut microbiota dysbiosis. The role of specific probiotic strains in mitigating these issues requires further clinical evidence. In a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, 72 H. pylori-positive patients received bismuth quadruple therapy alongside either Lactiplantibacillus plantarum Lp05 or a placebo for 2 weeks, followed by a 4-week extension of probiotic or placebo alone. Gastroin

papers.title: Characteristics of Anaerobic Bacteroides fragilis CMLF03 and Its Enzymes Involved in the Transformation of Panax notoginseng Saponins.
papers.abstract: Panax notoginseng saponins (PNS) are bioactive constituents that can be biotransformed by gut microbiota. Anaerobic Bacteroides bacteria are considered the next-generation probiotics. However, little is known about PNS biotransformation by Bacteroides bacteria. The study identified a nonenterotoxigenic Bacteroides fragilis capable of biotransforming PNS. Three family 3 glycoside hydrolases from the strain were heterologously expressed and identified as β-glucosidases GE-270 and GE-710 and β-xylo
papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: Fecal microbiota transplantation from Hezuo pigs alleviates intestinal inflammatory injury in Clostridium perfringens type C-infected piglets via modulation gut microbiota and intestinal barrier.
papers.abstract: Clostridium perfringens type C (CpC)-induced piglet diarrhea is a major challenge in the swine industry. This study aimed to investigate the protective effects of fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) from Hezuo pigs against intestinal injury in Duroc×Landrace×Yorkshire (DLY) suckling piglets infected with CpC. Piglets were divided into two groups, including CpC-challenged control group and FMT-treatment group, which received fecal suspension from Hezuo pigs prior to infection. Morphological, i

papers.title: High-resolution metabolomic analysis of stool reveals expanded biomarkers of C. difficile colitis and insights into pathophysiology.
papers.abstract: Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI) is facilitated by gut microbiome disruption and associated metabolic disturbances. While many prior studies have focused on the microbial composition of CDI stool, fewer have explored the fecal metabolome using untargeted high-resolution metabolomics (HRM). To characterize the metabolic phenotype of CDI, we performed untargeted HRM on stool samples from 55 CDI patients and 72 healthy controls. CDI was associated with marked alterations in stool metabolite

papers.title: Gut Microbiota and Autoimmune Diseases: The Therapeutic Potential of Probiotics.
papers.abstract: In recent years, increasing attention has been directed toward the role of gut microbiota in the pathogenesis of autoimmune diseases such as type 1 diabetes (T1D), rheumatoid arthritis (RA), and multiple sclerosis (MS). Numerous studies have shown that gut dysbiosis-an imbalance in the intestinal microbial community-can influence host immune responses by modulating inflammatory pathways, gut barrier integrity, and immune cell function. Patients with T1D, RA, and MS often exhibit a decreased abun

papers.title: Linkage of gut microbiota dysbiosis to chronic kidney disease in patients with graded proteinuria levels.
papers.abstract: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is highly prevalent worldwide, with proteinuria serving as a key diagnostic and prognostic indicator. The concept of the "gut-kidney axis" suggests that gut microbiota dysbiosis may contribute to CKD pathogenesis; however, its relationship with proteinuria severity remains unclear. papers.title: Akkermansia muciniphila-derived L-norleucine modulates FABP1-dependent fatty acid transport.
papers.abstract: Fatty acids undergo re-esterification to form triglycerides or are directly oxidized for energy production following absorption. Fatty acid binding protein 1 (FABP1), a key transporter highly expressed in both hepatic and intestinal tissues, directs the metabolic fate of absorbed fatty acids. Although its role in facilitating fatty acid transport and lipogenesis in the liver is well established, the functional mechanisms of intestinal FABP1 remain poorly understood due to the complexity of the i
papers.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

papers.title: Integrated multi-omics analysis reveals gut dysbiosis and altered energy metabolism in Chinese ALS patients.
papers.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a fatal neurodegenerative disorder with a complex etiology. Emerging evidence implicates gut microbiota dysbiosis in ALS pathology via the gut-brain axis, yet the specific integrative profile of the gut microbiome, virome, and metabolome, particularly in Chinese patients, remains incompletely characterized. Although global diversity indices showed no significant differences, taxonomic analysis revealed distinct compositional shifts. The ALS microbiome was c

papers.title: Individual Short-Chain Fatty Acids Differentially Reprogram Transcriptional States in Colorectal Cancer Cells.
papers.abstract: Short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) produced by the gut microbiota contribute to intestinal function, immune responses, and host metabolism, yet how individual SCFAs shape intracellular gene expression states remains incompletely defined. Here, we used intestinal epithelial-derived colorectal cancer cells to characterize early responses to sodium butyrate, propionate, valproate, acetate, and 3-hydroxybutyrate using RNA sequencing. RNA-seq showed that SCFA treatment altered transcriptional states, wi

papers.title: Early-Life Obesity Leaves a Metabolic Memory That Accelerates Aging-Related Decline Through the Gut Microbiota-GABA Axis.
papers.abstract: Childhood obesity is a critical public health concern. Whether diet-induced transient obesity during development negatively impacts later-life health remains unclear, and mechanisms are poorly understood. This study investigates whether these effects persist into aging and employs integrated omics to explore underlying mechanisms. Using a high-fat diet (HFD) to induce transient developmental obesity in post-weaning rats and larval Drosophila, we examined the long-term effects on aging metabolic

papers.title: Formula Probiotic Fermentation After Enzymatic Pretreatment Enhances Immunomodulatory Effects of Edible Seaweed Sargassum fusiforme via Gut Microbial Regulation.
papers.abstract: This study evaluated the efficacy of enzymatic pretreatment combined with formula probiotic fermentation in enhancing the structural and immunomodulatory properties of Sargassum fusiforme polysaccharides. Enzymatically pretreated and fermented polysaccharides (EFS) were obtained through enzymatic hydrolysis followed by fermentation with a multi-strain probiotic consortium (Lactobacillus plantarum, Lactobacillus acidophilus, and Bifidobacterium brevis).
